2025 · arXiv · Multimodal

Audio MultiChallenge: A Multi-Turn Evaluation of Spoken Dialogue Systems on Natural Human Interaction

Voice models look strong on clean, single-turn, synthetic speech — and fall apart in real conversation. Audio MultiChallenge is 452 unscripted multi-turn dialogues with 1,712 atomic rubrics, adding a new Voice Editing axis and audio-cue memory. The best system, Gemini 3 Pro Preview (Thinking), clears just 54.65%.

Advait Gosai*, Tyler Vuong*, Utkarsh Tyagi, Steven Li, Wenjia You, Miheer Bavare, Arda Uçar, Zhongwang Fang, Brian Jang, Bing Liu, Yunzhong He · Scale AI
venue arXiv citations 10 topic Multimodal source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Voice assistants are migrating from cascaded pipelines — automatic speech recognition → a text LLM → text-to-speech — to end-to-end (E2E) spoken dialogue systems that map input audio directly to a response over shared acoustic-and-text latents. The promise is lower latency, no error propagation across stages, and the preservation of paralinguistic and ambient signal that a transcript throws away. But the benchmarks that certify these systems lag the systems themselves: they mostly score single-turn tasks on synthetic or carefully read speech (VoiceBench, AudioBench, Big Bench Audio, MMAU-Pro, MMAR). Long-form audio suites (BLAB, AudioMarathon) stretch the clip but still treat each recording as one comprehension question.

That leaves the capability people actually use — extended, spontaneous conversation — almost untested in the audio modality. Text-only work already shows strong single-turn scores don't predict multi-turn robustness, and the problem is sharper in audio: speech is a continuous, high-frame-rate token stream that consumes context fast and makes long-horizon state tracking harder. The one prior speech-native multi-turn effort, MTalk-Bench, is limited to 2–3 scripted turns. And no speech benchmark at all tests a behavior unique to voice: spoken editing. In text you backspace a mistake before you send; in speech the correction is audible ("let's make it four yuccas, hmm no six"), and the model has to filter retracted content to recover the user's final intent.

Audio MultiChallenge targets that gap directly. It extends the text-based MultiChallenge framework (Inference Memory, Instruction Retention, Self Coherence) into audio, adds the new Voice Editing axis, and — crucially — replaces TTS-on-text with real human recordings so the dataset keeps the disfluencies, backchannels, cross-talk, accents, and ambient noise that text-derived audio cannot fake.

// how it works

The method

The dataset is 452 multi-turn conversations (3–8 turns each) from 47 unique speakers, totalling 14.99 hours of user audio recorded at 48 kHz with no post-processing, in English. It splits across the four axes — Inference Memory (132), Instruction Retention (120), Self Coherence (117), and Voice Editing (83) — and carries 1,712 atomic, instance-specific rubrics (≈3.8 per task).

Four axes, one audio-native twist each

Inference Memory asks the model to recall and synthesize scattered details that are implicitly required by the final request (remember a stated allergy when writing a recipe). The audio twist is Audio-Cue Gated memory: the critical context lives in the acoustic signal, not the words — heavy rain in turn 1 silently constraining an outfit request later. Instruction Retention broadens past stylistic constraints to stacked and conditional rules ("switch format only after I say X"). Self Coherence measures whether a model holds its ground instead of contradicting a correct earlier answer just because a user pushes back. Voice Editing — the new axis — tests robustness to mid-utterance self-corrections and edits that span turns, where the model must discard retracted content and resolve the user's true final intent.

A hybrid agentic + human-in-the-loop pipeline

Naturalistic, adversarial, diverse data is expensive, so curation is two-stage (Figure 3). First, an agentic blueprint generator: a Planner agent (OpenAI o3) sampling an axis/topic/persona devises a strategy to break a Target Audio LM; a Tester agent (GPT-4o) writes user turns, TTS'd with gpt-4o-mini-tts and fed to the target (GPT-4o Audio Preview or Gemini 2.5 Pro); the cycle escalates until a failure is found, then the Planner distills the trick into a human-friendly blueprint. Second, human contributors take that blueprint only as strategic guidance — they rewrite it in their own words, improvise, and record real audio against a live Audio LM until they manually induce a failure. (65% of conversations leveraged a synthetic blueprint.) On the failing final turn, contributors decompose the ideal response into atomic binary rubrics. A three-stage QC pass audits rubric quality (mutual-exclusivity, self-containment, accuracy, atomicity) and overall quality (audio quality, axis-specificity, contrivedness).

Fixed-context grading with two metrics

Evaluation is fixed-context (Figure 4): every model receives the identical pre-seeded conversation history — user turns as base64 audio, assistant turns as text — and is graded only on its response to the final turn. This avoids the conversation drift of turn-by-turn replay (a slightly off response invalidates later pre-recorded follow-ups) and guarantees a reproducible, apples-to-apples context. An LLM-as-a-judge (o4-mini) then scores the text stream of the response against the human rubrics. Two metrics follow: the strict Average Pass Rate (APR) — a task counts only if all its rubrics pass — and the softer Average Rubric Score (ARS), the mean fraction of rubrics satisfied.

APR = 1N Σi 𝟙[ all rubrics of task i satisfied ]   ·   ARS = 1N Σi satisfieditotali // APR = all-or-nothing pass rate (the headline score); ARS = average rubric compliance
// what they found

The results

Across ~20 model/output configurations, the benchmark is hard for everyone. Gemini 3 Pro Preview (Thinking) leads at 54.65% APR (82.54% ARS), trailed by Gemini 2.5 Pro Thinking (46.90%) and 2.5 Flash Thinking (40.04%). Everything else is bunched far lower: the best non-Gemini systems are Voxtral Small 24B (26.33%), GPT-4o Audio Preview (25.44%, text), Qwen 3 Omni (24.34%), and GPT Realtime (23.45%, text). Open-source S2S models struggle most, bottoming out at LFM2 Audio 1.5B's 9.29%. Per axis, the two audio-native additions are the walls: Voice Editing averages 17.99% (hardest) and Inference Memory 21.55%.

Model (output)APR %ARS %VE %
Gemini 3 Pro Preview (Thinking) · text54.6582.5440.96
Gemini 2.5 Pro (Thinking) · text46.9078.9140.96
Gemini 2.5 Flash (Thinking) · text40.0474.5954.22
Voxtral Small 24B · text26.3366.5933.33
GPT-4o Audio Preview · text25.4467.5821.37
Qwen 3 Omni · audio24.3463.4321.37
GPT Realtime · text23.4565.9522.22
Kimi Audio 7B Instruct · audio10.4022.085.98
LFM2 Audio 1.5B · audio9.2919.100.00

Audio-cue memory is the sharpest deficit. On the 42 Audio-Cue Gated tasks — where the fact to recall lives only in tone or background sound, never the words — ARS falls 36.5% relative versus semantic memory, averaged across models (Figure 5). Models are often bottlenecked at perception (reliably hearing the cue) before retention even matters.

Self Coherence decays with context — measured by audio, not turns. Turn count barely moves scores (the conversations are short relative to context windows, and the audited history doubles as in-context examples). But cumulative user-audio duration is a real proxy for effective context, and on it Self Coherence drops from 33.3% (0–60 s) to 20.0% (3–5 min) to 13.3% beyond 5 min (n=3). Voice Editing collapses even faster with duration — 17.5% in the 3–5 min bin to 1.2% and 0% beyond (n=4, n=2). Instruction Retention, by contrast, holds or improves, because early conditional/fixed constraints get reinforced by the audited prior assistant turns.

Other findings: a "modality gap" — the same models drop from 19.36% (text output) to 16.76% (audio output), so emitting speech costs accuracy; and conditional instructions are reliably harder than fixed ones for every model (Instruction Retention averages 25.08% APR / 46.11% ARS). The o4-mini judge agrees with humans at Cohen's κ 0.873 / Macro-F1 0.937 (≈93%).

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The data-collection bar here is unusually high — real recordings, an adversarial human-in-the-loop loop, atomic rubrics, a meta-validated judge, and a clean set of ablations (TTS-vs-human, E2E-vs-cascaded). The honest tension is between the paper's framing — a benchmark for spoken dialogue systems — and what it actually grades.

It grades the transcript, not the voice. The judge reads only the text stream of the response (Figure 4), and the authors name this as their key limitation: evaluation "does not operate directly on raw audio outputs." So prosody, naturalness, latency, and turn-taking — the things that distinguish a good spoken assistant — go unscored. Tellingly, the headline 54.65% is a text-output config, and the modality gap (19.36% → 16.76%) shows audio output is penalized. The leaderboard therefore ranks audio understanding + text reasoning more than end-to-end spoken-dialogue quality.

How much "audio-cue" credit survives in a transcript? A deaf cascade (Whisper ASR + GPT-5) scores 35.82% on the audio-cue/interruption subset (n=67), and even GPT-4o cascaded reaches 23.88%. The authors' read — that reasoners infer paralinguistic context from transcripts — is plausible, but it also means a meaningful share of "audio-native" credit is reachable without hearing the audio (Whisper transcribes disfluencies and interruptions). The cleanest version of this axis would isolate tasks that no transcript can solve.

Some headline trends ride on thin bins. The Self Coherence ">5 min" point is n=3; the Voice-Editing duration collapse rests on bins of n=4 and n=2. And while the authors add exclusion rubrics to deter verbosity-hacking, ARS still tracks response length almost linearly — the only rebuttal is a manual review of the Gemini family, a single-family subjective check rather than a control applied across all 20 systems.

baselines

Broad and current: ~20 configs spanning Gemini 3 Pro / 2.5, GPT Realtime, GPT-4o(/mini) Audio Preview, Qwen 2.5/3 Omni, Voxtral, Mimo, Phi-4 MM, Gemma 3n, Kimi Audio, LFM2 — plus ASR+LLM cascades (GPT-5, Opus 4.5) and TTS ablations. Fair coverage of the frontier and the open field.

contamination

Low leakage risk — a brand-new (Dec 2025) corpus of unscripted human recordings, not web-scraped, graded on the final turn only. The real bias is selection: conversations are kept only when a human broke GPT-4o Audio Preview or Gemini 2.5 Pro, so the set is adversarially tuned to those two collectors (which partly flatters Gemini at the top).

what's missed

The spoken output itself — prosody, naturalness, latency, turn-taking — is never graded (transcript-only). Audio-cue credit is partially obtainable from a transcript. Score correlates with verbosity despite exclusion rubrics.

would raise confidence

Open-ended judging on the raw audio output; an audio-cue slice provably unsolvable from any transcript; per-model judge agreement that includes weak open S2S models; larger duration bins; a systematic length control across all models; and a non-adversarial held-out slice.

// bottom line

A genuinely harder, more naturalistic voice benchmark, and the Voice Editing axis + audio-cue memory are real, unmet capabilities — even Gemini 3 Pro stalls at 54.65%. But read the score for what it is: an upper bound on multi-turn understanding graded from the response transcript under a generous fixed-context setup, not a measurement of how good these systems sound when they actually speak. The paper says as much in its limitations — the next benchmark has to grade the audio.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Real multi-turn voice breaks frontier models. 452 unscripted human conversations + 1,712 atomic rubrics, and the best system — Gemini 3 Pro Preview (Thinking) — passes only 54.65% (strict all-rubrics APR); open S2S models sink toward 9%.
  2. The audio-native axes are the walls. Voice Editing (mid-utterance repairs, prior-turn edits) is hardest at 17.99% avg; audio-cue memory trails semantic memory by 36.5% relative; and Self Coherence decays with cumulative audio (33.3% → 20.0%), not with turn count.
  3. It measures the words, not the voice. Grading reads the response transcript, the headline scores are text-output configs (audio output costs ~3 pts), and a deaf ASR+LLM cascade still scores ~36% on the "audio-cue" subset — so the next step is open-ended, audio-native evaluation.
